raw_case:
Novel Use of Metagenomic Next-Generation Sequencing in Diagnosing Streptococcus intermedius Meningitis and Ventriculitis: A Case Report and Literature Review


Background

Streptococcus intermedius is a commensal microflora commonly found in various mucosal sites in the respiratory, gastrointestinal, and genitourinary tracts. It causes invasive suppurative infections including liver and brain abscesses along with thoracic empyema. However, it rarely causes meningitis without abscess formation.

Case Presentation

A 56-year-old immunocompetent man who presented with fever and headache. Bacterial meningitis was confirmed using cerebrospinal fluid analysis. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain revealed leptomeningitis and ventriculitis. However, conventional methods, such as microbiological culture failed to identify the causative pathogens. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing of cerebrospinal fluid revealed the presence of S. intermedius. It allowed us the optimal treatment for him. The patient underwent antibiotic treatment with 6-week duration of ceftriaxone administration accompanied by surgical intervention, resulting in a favorable prognosis.

Conclusion

Herein, we report a rare case of meningitis and ventriculitis caused by S. intermedius using metagenomic next-generation sequencing. The patient recovered well after antibiotic treatment and surgery. We present this rare case and summarize previous studies to remind clinicians that timely identification of the pathogen and optimal treatment are crucial for management of S. intermedius-induced infections.

Introduction

Central nervous system (CNS) infection is a life-threatening infectious disease that usually results in high morbidity and mortality.1,2 Accurate and timely identification of the causative pathogen is important for effective management of CNS infections.2 Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) culture is the gold standard for the diagnosis of CNS infection and to guide appropriate antimicrobial therapy. However, the causative pathogen for CNS infection is not identified in approximately half of patients,3,4 accounting for poor patient outcomes and higher economic burdens to the health care system.3Streptococcus intermedius is a human commensal microflora belonging to the Streptococcus anginosus group (SAG).5 It is associated with dental plaque and usually causes complicated invasive suppurative infections at various body sites, resulting in brain abscesses, dentoalveolar infections, infective endocarditis, and liver abscesses.5,6 This pathogen is mostly isolated from the pus of infection foci instead of from non-pyogenic lesions.6 However, identification of S. intermedius is difficult because of a lack of reliable phenotypic characteristics among SAG members. Therefore, a PCR amplification with multi-locus sequence analysis would be a more reliable tool to pathogen identification. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) has been used as a rapid and reliable tool for identifying microorganisms that exhibits promising detection ability in patients with infectious diseases, including meningitis.3,4,7–9 It can identify a broad spectrum of pathogens, including both commonly and rarely reported causative agents of CNS infections.7 In this report, we describe an immunocompetent man with meningitis and ventriculitis caused by S. intermedius, which was detected by the mNGS technique using a culture-negative CSF sample. Additionally, we have summarized previously reported cases of S. intermedius meningitis. The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Tri-Service General Hospital, which also consented to the publication of the case details.(TSGHIRB No.:C202415030).

Case Presentation

A 56-year-old Taiwanese male with a history of hypertension and dyslipidemia was taking regular medications. He presented to a hospital emergency department in December 2023 with headache, chills, nuchal rigidity, and nausea over 3-d. His vital signs showed body temperature of 38.7 °C, rapid heart rate of 112 beats/min, normal respiratory rate of 18/min, and normal blood pressure of 114/63 mmHg. Neurological examination revealed as the follows: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) E4V5M6, cognitively intact and orientation, fluent speech, steady gait, free and conjugated extraocular movement, isochoric pupils with a light reflex, positive Kernig’s sign, absent Babinski sign, and intact motor or sensory function. Laboratory results disclosed remarkable leukocytosis (white blood cell [WBC] count: 32,460/μL; normal reference range: 4500–11,000/μL) and elevated C-reactive protein level (18.58 mg/dL; normal reference range: <0.8 mg/dL). Influenza virus antigen (Ag) and SARS-CoV-2 rapid Ag test results were negative. Computed tomography of the brain revealed no remarkable findings. Lumbar puncture was performed at an opening pressure of 17 cmH2O to obtain the CSF sample, which appeared turbid and yellowish. CSF analysis revealed the following: WBC 1687/μL, glucose <10 mg/dL (normal reference range: 40–70 mg/dL), and total protein 772 mg/dL (normal reference range: 15–45 mg/dL). Empirical antibiotics, including ceftriaxone 2g and vancomycin 1g twice per day, were administered to treat probable bacterial meningitis. The CSF was negative for Group B Streptococcus Ag, Neisseria meningitidis Ag, S. pneumoniae Ag, Hemophilus influenza Ag, India Ink stain, Cryptococcus Ag test, and PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex. The results of a BIOFIRE® FILMARRAY® Meningitis/Encephalitis Panel also demonstrated negative results. The CSF fluid and blood cultures yielded no pathogen. Furthermore, CSF, obtained through a repeat lumbar puncture on the 7th d of hospitalization, was sent for mNGS. The results discovered the presence of the S. intermedius gene sequences (Table 1).
Contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging of the brain revealed leptomeningeal enhancement in the bilateral cerebral sulci and brainstem surface, suggestive of meningitis and subependymal enhancement with sedimentation in the bilateral lateral ventricles (Figure 1). The patient was diagnosed with S. intermedius meningitis and ventriculitis, and treated with ceftriaxone administration and undergoing external ventricular drainage (EVD). Follow-up CSF analysis revealed the following: WBC 7/μL, glucose 61 mg/dL, and total protein 67 mg/dL. EVD was transitioned to a ventriculoperitoneal shunt for continuous drainage. The patient was discharged 6 weeks post-treatment without neurological sequelae.


Discussion

In this study, we reported the case of an immunocompetent man with S. intermedius infection presenting with meningitis and ventriculitis. The pathogen was identified in CSF samples using mNGS. The patient recovered well after antibiotic treatment and surgery.Meningitis is a life-threatening infectious disease and accounts for an estimated 2.51 million cases annually and up to 236,000 deaths globally.10 The overall mortality rate of bacterial meningitis was 16.4% in adults, and increased to 22.7% in the elderly (≥ 65 years) in the United States.1 CSF culture is the gold standard for pathogen identification in CNS infections,2,11,12 however, the positive rate of CSF cultures was estimated to be only 30–40% in CNS infections.7 Patients who received antibiotic treatment > 6 h after disease presentation had higher in-hospital mortality and worse outcomes at discharge, compared to those who received treatment within 2 h of admission.13 Although timely antibiotic treatment for CNS infections management is important, the usage of antibiotics before CSF sampling would decrease pathogen detection rates.7,12,14 While S. intermedius is vulnerable to β-lactam antibiotics, antibiotic treatment before obtaining CSF samples can lower detection efficacy, which clarifies reason for the negative results of CSF pathogen detection tests in this case.SAGs represent normal flora and can mostly be isolated from dental plaques and mucosa of respiratory, gastrointestinal, and urogenital tracts in human beings.6,15 The incidence of SAG bacteremia has increased gradually in recent years.16 As a member of SAG, S. intermedius has high mortality rates, longer hospital stays, and usually causes suppurative infections, such as brain abscess, liver abscesses, and thoracic empyema.6,17,18 Several virulence factors, such as antigens I/II surface proteins, hyaluronidase, and polysaccharidic capsule have been known as contributors of abscess formation by S. intermedius.6 This may be the reason that S. intermedius-induced CNS infections usually present as brain abscess rather than meningitis. Although, several cases of S. intermedius brain abscesses have been documented, only four cases of S. intermedius meningitis or ventriculitis have been previously reported (Table 2). Four of the five involved individuals were over the age of 60, while the fourth case was a 6-year-old boy. Among them, there was no specific risk factors for S. intermedius meningitis or ventriculitis been recognized, such as dental operation, sinusitis, or diabetes mellitus.6 CSF cultures yielded negative results in two of the reported five cases. The percentage of CSF culture positive was two in these five cases. The S. intermedius were identified by PCR amplification or mNGS in the culture-negative cases. As previous documented that SAGs are susceptible to β-lactam antibiotics and display less antibiotic resistance,19 all the reported patients survived after 2 to 6 weeks duration of β-lactam antibiotics treatment.20–23
Optimal and adequate sampling for pathogen identification before antibiotic administration is important in the management of CNS infections. While conventional microbiologic testing is insufficient to detect all pathogens, mNGS exhibits satisfactory diagnostic performance in CNS infections and has a higher detection rate than conventional culture techniques.24 The application of mNGS can improve the diagnosis of CNS infections, with a sensitivity and specificity of 73 and 99%, respectively.25 Furthermore, DNA or RNA sequencing of pathogens via mNGS accelerates turnaround time in testing, which ranges from approximately 6 h to 7 d (average of 2 d).

Conclusion

We report a rare case of S. intermedius infection presenting with meningitis and cerebral ventriculitis. In this case, no pathogen was identified using conventional diagnostic methods, including CSF culture, pathogen Ag testing, and a Filmarray multiplex PCR panel. Using the mNGS technique, CSF samples discovered DNA sequences of S. intermedius, confirming the diagnosis; thus, verifying the therapeutic strategy. As a cutting-edge technology, mNGS has a significantly high-detection sensitivity, and can be applied for the detection of unusual pathogens in CNS infections.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 56-year-old Taiwanese male.
- Chief Complaint: Headache, chills, nuchal rigidity, and nausea for 3 days.
- History of Present Illness: Acute presentation of symptoms consistent with meningitis.
- Past Medical History: Hypertension and dyslipidemia, managed with regular medications.
- Personal History: None reported.
- Family History: None reported.
- Physical Examination: Fever (38.7°C), rapid heart rate (112 bpm), nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig’s sign; neurological examination largely unremarkable with Glasgow Coma Scale (E4V5M6).
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Blood tests: Leukocytosis (WBC = 32,460/μL), elevated C-reactive protein (18.58 mg/dL).
  - CSF analysis: WBC 1687/μL, glucose <10 mg/dL, total protein 772 mg/dL; turbid and yellowish appearance.
  - CSF pathogen tests: Negative for bacterial antigens, fungal markers, and PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
  - Imaging: Magnetic resonance imaging showed leptomeningitis and ventriculitis; CT scan was normal.
  - Microbiology: Initial CSF and repeat CSF culture-negative.
  - Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS): Identified Streptococcus intermedius.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential**: The patient's presentation of fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, leukocytosis, and abnormal CSF findings (elevated WBC, low glucose, high protein) strongly suggested bacterial meningitis. The presence of ventriculitis on imaging broadened the differential to include:
   - Common bacterial pathogens: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae.
   - Viral meningitis: Ruled out as viral antigen tests were negative.
   - Fungal meningitis (e.g., Cryptococcus): Ruled out by negative India ink stain and Cryptococcus antigen.
   - Tuberculous meningitis: Unlikely given negative CSF PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
   - Atypical bacterial pathogens (e.g., Streptococcus intermedius): Considered due to failure of culture methods and imaging findings.

2. **Step-wise Exclusion**:
   - Negative antigen and PCR panel results excluded common bacterial and fungal pathogens.
   - The negative India ink and lack of immunocompromise ruled out Cryptococcus species.
   - Negative blood and CSF cultures pointed toward atypical or challenging-to-cultivate bacteria.

3. **Diagnostic Confirmation**:
   - mNGS detected Streptococcus intermedius DNA in the CSF, confirming its presence even though traditional culture methods had failed.
   - Imaging findings of leptomeningitis and ventriculitis aligned with S. intermedius infection.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Streptococcus intermedius meningitis and ventriculitis. The diagnosis was confirmed via metagenomic next-generation sequencing of the CSF. The patient's favorable response to ceftriaxone treatment and surgical intervention further supported the diagnosis.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaint: headache, chills, nuchal rigidity, and nausea for 3 days. These symptoms are classic signs of meningitis. The acute presentation aligns with that, so I need to confirm if it's bacterial, viral, or something else.

The patient is a 56-year-old male from Taiwan with a history of hypertension and dyslipidemia, but those are managed. No significant personal or family history. So, maybe his immune system isn't compromised, but age could be a factor.

Physical exam shows fever, high heart rate, nuchal rigidity, and positive Kernig's sign. These are strong indicators of meningeal irritation. The GCS is 15 (E4V5M6), so he's alert, which is a bit better than some severe bacterial cases but not ruling it out.

Blood tests show leukocytosis (WBC 32k) and elevated CRP. That's a big inflammatory response, pointing towards infection. CSF analysis has high WBC (1687), low glucose, high protein, and turbid yellowish fluid. These CSF findings are typical for bacterial meningitis. The low glucose and high protein suggest bacterial rather than viral.

Imaging: MRI shows leptomeningitis and ventriculitis, but CT was normal. MRI findings support the diagnosis of meningitis, especially with ventriculitis, which can occur in bacterial cases.

Now, the tricky part is the CSF cultures and initial pathogen tests. They were negative for bacterial antigens, fungi, and TB PCR. But the metagenomic next-gen sequencing found Streptococcus intermedius. That's a key clue. Culture-negative meningitis can happen if prior antibiotics were given, but the mNGS is more sensitive and specific here.

Streptococcus intermedius is part of the S. anginosus group, which can cause abscesses and CNS infections. It's a gram-positive coccus, often found in oral flora. So, possible source could be dental procedures, endocarditis, or other infections. But the patient's history doesn't mention recent procedures, so maybe another source.

Since all other tests were negative except mNGS, and the CSF profile fits bacterial, the diagnosis would be Streptococcus intermedius meningitis. Even though cultures are negative, mNGS is reliable here. The treatment would be appropriate antibiotics targeting that pathogen.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with acute meningitis symptoms (headache, nuchal rigidity, fever) and signs (positive Kernig’s sign, CSF pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose). CSF turbidity and leukocytosis strongly suggest bacterial meningitis.  
<step 2> CSF cultures and bacterial antigen tests are negative, but mNGS identified Streptococcus intermedius. Culture negativity may result from prior antibiotic use or fastidious growth requirements, but mNGS has high specificity for pathogen detection.  
<step 3> Streptococcus intermedius, part of the Streptococcus anginosus group, is a known cause of pyogenic CNS infections, including meningitis and brain abscesses, often associated with contiguous or hematogenous spread from occult foci.  
<step 4> The combination of clinical findings (ventriculitis, CSF profile), absence of other pathogens, and mNGS confirmation solidifies Streptococcus intermedius as the definitive etiology.  

### Answer:
Streptococcus intermedius meningitis

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with acute meningitis symptoms (headache, nuchal rigidity, fever) and signs (positive Kernig’s sign, CSF pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose). CSF turbidity and leukocytosis strongly suggest bacterial meningitis.

CSF cultures and bacterial antigen tests are negative, but mNGS identified Streptococcus intermedius. Culture negativity may result from prior antibiotic use or fastidious growth requirements, but mNGS has high specificity for pathogen detection.

Streptococcus intermedius, part of the Streptococcus anginosus group, is a known cause of pyogenic CNS infections, including meningitis and brain abscesses, often associated with contiguous or hematogenous spread from occult foci.
The combination of clinical findings (ventriculitis, CSF profile), absence of other pathogens, and mNGS confirmation solidifies Streptococcus intermedius as the definitive etiology.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: The patient's symptoms (fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, leukocytosis, abnormal CSF findings) and imaging (ventriculitis) suggest bacterial meningitis, specifically considering Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae, and atypical bacteria like Streptococcus intermedius.
Step 2: Viral meningitis is ruled out due to negative viral antigen tests.
Step 3: Fungal meningitis (Cryptococcus) is ruled out by negative India ink stain and Cryptococcus antigen test.
Step 4: Tuberculous meningitis is unlikely given negative CSF PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Step 5: Atypical bacterial pathogens (e.g., Streptococcus intermedius) are considered due to imaging findings and failure of culture methods.
Step 6: Negative antigen and PCR panel results exclude common bacterial and fungal pathogens.
Step 7: Negative India ink stain and lack of immunocompromise rule out Cryptococcus species.
Step 8: Negative blood and CSF cultures point towards atypical or challenging-to-cultivate bacteria.
Step 9: mNGS detects Streptococcus intermedius DNA in the CSF, confirming its presence.
Step 10: Imaging findings of leptomeningitis and ventriculitis align with S. intermedius infection; the diagnosis is confirmed via metagenomic next-generation sequencing of the CSF.
